author: closure_king
content:
A closure is just a function that remembers the variables around it when it was born, even after that outer function returns.
Think of it like a backpack: the inner function packs the variables it needs and carries them everywhere.
This is why your click handler still knows the `count` from the function that set it up.

comments.author: proto_chain
comments.content: The backpack metaphor finally made this click for me. Bonus: this is exactly how React's useState callback closes over a stale value if you forget the functional updater.

comments.author: event_looper
comments.content: Worth adding: each call to counter() makes a fresh backpack, so two counters never share their count.

author: event_looper
content:
JavaScript runs on one thread, like a single cashier. setTimeout doesn't hire a second cashier — it puts your task at the back of the line.
That's why `setTimeout(fn, 0)` doesn't run immediately. It waits until the current line of customers (your synchronous code) is done.
The event loop is just the manager waving the next customer forward.

comments.author: async_await
comments.content: And promises jump a shorter queue (microtasks) that fully drains before any setTimeout callback runs. So a resolved promise logs before that timeout 0.

author: type_narrow
content:
Common interview trap: "What's the difference between TypeScript types and runtime checks?"
Types are erased at compile time. They are gone before your code ever runs, like guardrails a compiler removes before shipping.
So `if (typeof x === 'string')` survives to runtime, but a type annotation does not. If you need a check at runtime, write actual JS.

comments.author: closure_king
comments.content: This is the whole reason Zod and similar libs exist: TS can't validate API responses for you because the types vanish. You still parse at the boundary.

comments.author: proto_chain
comments.content: I bombed this exact question once. The phrase 'types are erased' is the answer they want to hear.

author: async_await
content:
Spot the bug: this loop is supposed to log 0, 1, 2 but logs 3, 3, 3.
The culprit is `var` — it's function-scoped, so all three callbacks close over the same single `i`.
Swap `var` for `let` and each iteration gets its own binding. Same fix you'd reach for in any closure-in-a-loop gotcha.

comments.author: closure_king
comments.content: Classic. Before let existed people wrapped the body in an IIFE to capture i by value. let just bakes that fresh-binding behavior in for you.

comments.author: event_looper
comments.content: And it logs 3 not 2 because the loop finishes incrementing i to 3 before any timeout fires. Event loop strikes again.

author: proto_chain
content:
Prototypes are JavaScript's version of inheritance, but it's lookup, not copying.
When you call `arr.map(...)`, JS doesn't find map on your array, so it walks up the chain to Array.prototype and finds it there.
If you came from Java or Python classes: it's like every object having a pointer to its parent, and method calls bubble up that pointer chain until they hit a match or null.

comments.author: type_narrow
comments.content: And `class` in modern JS is just sugar over this. extends sets up the prototype link, super walks up the chain. Same machinery underneath.

author: async_await
content:
Stop reaching for try/catch around every await. Wrap the unit of work, not each line.
If three awaits belong to one operation, one try/catch communicates "this whole thing can fail" far more clearly.
And remember: an unhandled rejection in an async function is a thrown error to whoever awaits it — it doesn't silently vanish.

comments.author: event_looper
comments.content: If those three awaits don't depend on each other, Promise.all runs them concurrently instead of waiting in sequence. Big latency win.

author: type_narrow
content:
Hot take: `any` is worse than no types at all.
With plain JS you stay alert because you know nothing is checked. With `any` you get a false green light — the compiler shrugs and lets bugs through while you feel safe.
Reach for `unknown` instead. It forces you to prove what the value is before you touch it.

comments.author: async_await
comments.content: Hard agree. Turn on noImplicitAny so stray anys can't sneak in from untyped imports either.

comments.author: closure_king
comments.content: The 'false green light' framing is perfect. any doesn't disable a check, it lies about one passing.

author: event_looper
content:
TIL await doesn't block the thread — it pauses your function and hands control back to the event loop.
Under the hood, the rest of your async function is essentially scheduled as a microtask callback once the promise settles. It's closures again: the paused function remembers its local state.
So while you 'wait', other code keeps running. The cashier never stops serving the line.

comments.author: proto_chain
comments.content: This is why async/await felt like magic until it didn't. It's just a state machine over promises that the engine resumes when the microtask runs.

author: proto_chain
content:
If you're coming from Python, `===` is your `==`, and JS `==` is the weird one.
`==` coerces types before comparing, so `0 == ''` is true and `null == undefined` is true. That coercion table is a footgun.
Use `===` everywhere and the rule becomes simple: same type and same value, no surprises.
code:
0 == '';        // true  (coerced)
0 === '';       // false
null == undefined;  // true
null === undefined; // false

comments.author: type_narrow
comments.content: The one pragmatic exception people allow: x == null to catch both null and undefined in one shot. Otherwise === always.

author: closure_king
content:
Thread: how a debounce works, and why it's just a closure.
1/ A debounce delays running a function until the user stops triggering it — like waiting for someone to finish typing before you search.
2/ The trick is remembering the pending timer between calls. That memory lives in a closure over `timer`.
3/ Each new call clears the old timer and sets a fresh one, so only the last call survives.

comments.author: event_looper
comments.content: Nice tie-in: clearTimeout cancels the pending macrotask before the event loop ever gets to it. The closure holds the handle that lets you cancel.

comments.author: async_await
comments.content: Throttle is the sibling worth learning next: run at most once per interval instead of waiting for silence. Same closure pattern, different timing logic.
